exec.title: NASSCOM-Zinnov: India GCC ecosystem hits $98.4B revenue, 2,117 centers
exec.body: The NASSCOM-Zinnov 'GCC Value Orbit' report released July 3 shows India now hosts 2,117 GCCs across 3,728 units employing 2.36 million professionals with total market revenue of $98.4 billion. The ecosystem has grown 32% since FY2021, with 506 Forbes Global 2000 companies operating GCCs in India. Nearly 50% of GCCs now operate at high maturity, with almost half of all GCCs established since FY2021 built with AI as a core mandate from inception.
exec.why: Strategic Implication: The 'delivery engine to enterprise nerve centre' transition is now statistically validated—GCC leaders who have not yet shifted P&L accountability and AI governance ownership to India are behind the majority of the ecosystem.

exec.title: Deloitte: Japan becomes largest APAC contributor with 100+ India GCCs
exec.body: A Deloitte India report released July 3 finds Japanese enterprises have surpassed all other APAC nations in India GCC presence with 100+ centres accounting for 5–6% of India's overall GCC ecosystem. Japanese GCCs are evolving from support functions into high-value hubs spanning AI, embedded systems, cloud, advanced analytics, and digital manufacturing. The sector mix is led by technology (20%), industrials (15%), and automotive and healthcare (11% each), with Deloitte projecting the GCC sector will generate US$470–600 billion in net economic impact by FY2030.
exec.why: Strategic Implication: Non-US, non-European parent companies are rapidly accelerating India GCC mandates; GCC leaders should benchmark capability expansion plans against this new competitive baseline from APAC-headquartered peers.

exec.title: Bihar unveils GCC Policy 2026 at Bengaluru IT Meet, targets Oracle, Salesforce, Honeywell
exec.body: Bihar hosted an invitation-only IT Industry Meet on July 3 in Bengaluru, unveiling the Bihar GCC Policy 2026, Bihar AI Policy 2026, and a Rs 25,000 crore technology infrastructure plan. Closed-door sessions were held with senior leadership at Adobe, Honeywell, Oracle, Salesforce, and Mphasis. The state is positioning itself as an emerging GCC destination with competitive incentives and a growing engineering talent pool outside metros.
exec.why: Strategic Implication: State-level GCC policy competition is intensifying—GCC leaders evaluating expansion or secondary sites now have a broader set of policy incentives to leverage in location strategy negotiations.

exec.title: India sees fresh wave of marquee GCC setups in July: State Street, Costco, Albertsons, Alight
exec.body: India witnessed a fresh wave of marquee GCC setups and large-scale expansions in July 2026. State Street opened a 210,000 sq ft facility in Coimbatore capable of employing 2,400 people. US retail giant Costco established its first India GCC in Hyderabad targeting 1,000 professionals. Albertsons expanded its Bengaluru GCC with plans to hire 1,000 tech professionals over 18 months for AI and data-driven retail innovation. Consulting firm Alight Solutions launched a new GCC in Chennai for HR and financial capabilities.
exec.why: Strategic Implication: Retail and financial sector GCCs entering India via tier-II cities signals that the talent and cost proposition is compelling even outside Bengaluru/Hyderabad—existing GCC leaders should review their own city diversification strategies.

exec.title: Zurich Insurance launches Hyderabad GCC for AI transformation, names Amit Kalra global head
exec.body: Zurich Insurance Group launched a Global Capability Centre in Hyderabad on July 1, 2026, appointing Amit Kalra as Head of Zurich Capability Centres with responsibility for all global locations. The center will serve as an extension of Zurich's global operating model with end-to-end ownership across cloud and platform engineering, data and AI, application development, and cybersecurity. Teams will embed AI-enabled ways of working from day one, contributing directly to global platforms and solutions.
exec.why: Strategic Implication: Zurich's decision to place the global head of all capability centres in India underscores the elevation of India GCC leadership to board-level strategic roles—a benchmark for GCC leaders advocating for expanded mandates.

themes.title: 83% of India GCCs now scaling GenAI; 58% investing in Agentic AI capabilities
themes.body: The NASSCOM-Zinnov GCC Landscape Report 2026 finds 83% of India GCCs are scaling GenAI and 58% are actively investing in Agentic AI. GenAI applications are concentrated in customer service (65%), finance (53%), operations (49%), and IT/cybersecurity (45%). GCC maturity has shifted from capability-focused execution to architecting global decisions, AI governance, and enterprise-wide standards, with leading GCCs transitioning to a hybrid agentic operating model where autonomous AI handles continuous execution while human teams set strategic intent.
themes.why: Strategic Implication: GCCs still in GenAI pilot mode are now an outlier; the benchmark is scaled deployment with measurable ROI targets—leaders should audit current AI investment levels against the 83%/58% adoption curve.

themes.title: AI and data skills now drive 2 in 3 new GCC hires as total hiring rises 11%
themes.body: NASSCOM-Zinnov data shows that nearly two in three new GCC roles created in 2026 require AI, data science, or intelligent automation skills, with AI-centric skills demand rising 1.5 percentage points in the past six months. Overall GCC hiring has risen 11% year-on-year. Volume hiring is giving way to a skills-first approach where niche AI and cloud roles command 1.7x salary premiums and one in four GCC positions will be contractual by year-end.
themes.why: Strategic Implication: GCC talent strategies built around headcount growth must urgently pivot to skills-density models; compensation structures that do not differentiate AI/data roles from general engineering roles will lose top talent to peers who do.

themes.title: EY: Agentic AI GCCs—24% of enterprise tasks fully automatable, 42% significantly augmentable
themes.body: EY India analysis finds that 24% of enterprise tasks across GCC functions are fully automatable using agentic AI, with another 42% significantly augmentable. In finance functions specifically, autonomous agentic systems are reducing process cycle times by up to 70%. 92% of GCC leaders affirm their centres contribute far beyond cost arbitrage, with the transition from labour-cost arbitrage to capability arbitrage now well underway. Finance and Customer Experience functions are expected to see the highest agentic AI impact by 2026.
themes.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders should immediately quantify the 24%+42% automation opportunity within their own function mix and present a redeployment roadmap to global leadership—failure to lead this transition invites externally imposed headcount decisions.

themes.title: India GCCs accelerating shift to AI-native enterprises with full lifecycle AI ownership
themes.body: EY India's GCC analysis shows Indian GCCs are taking on full lifecycle ownership of AI systems, platforms, and business outcomes for global enterprises. The shift is from delivery execution to enterprise nerve centres, with GCCs responsible for AI governance frameworks, enterprise-wide standards, and product mandates. Nearly half of all GCCs established since FY2021 were built with AI as a core function from day one, and 96% of post-2021 GCCs launched with a product or portfolio mandate.
themes.why: Strategic Implication: GCCs without explicit AI governance charters and enterprise-wide AI standards are falling behind peers who have embedded these responsibilities—establishing an India-based AI Centre of Excellence is now a competitive necessity, not a future-state aspiration.

themes.title: ORF: India GCCs positioned to lead global white-collar work transformation in AI era
themes.body: ORF's research paper finds India's 2,100+ GCCs are uniquely positioned to lead the global transformation of white-collar work as AI displaces routine cognitive tasks. GCCs that combine India's deep digital talent pool, AI deployment experience, and operating knowledge at scale are being leveraged for innovation arbitrage, not just cost arbitrage. The research highlights that over 90% of leading GCCs co-innovate with universities and more than half engage with startups through open sandboxes and tech pilots.
themes.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders should build university partnership and startup co-innovation pipelines now—these external ecosystems are becoming core differentiators in attracting top AI talent and demonstrating strategic value to global headquarters.

deals.title: State Street opens 210,000 sq ft Coimbatore facility with capacity for 2,400 jobs
deals.body: State Street has opened a new 210,000 sq ft, four-floor facility at India Land Tech Park in Coimbatore with capacity for approximately 2,400 employees, making a significant strategic bet on a tier-II city. The move follows an earlier Chennai GCC launch weeks prior, establishing a multi-city South India footprint. Coimbatore joins Accenture, Hexaware, Amazon, Bosch, and Cognizant already operating from the same tech park, cementing the city's emergence as India's next GCC growth corridor.
deals.why: Strategic Implication: BFSI GCCs willing to anchor tier-II cities can access lower attrition and emerging engineering talent pools—State Street's Coimbatore expansion offers a replicable blueprint for financial sector GCC leaders evaluating secondary sites.

deals.title: Costco establishes first India GCC in Hyderabad HITEC City, plans 1,000 jobs
deals.body: US retail giant Costco is establishing its first Global Capability Centre in India, choosing Hyderabad's HITEC City for a technology and research hub expected to employ 1,000 professionals initially. The centre will focus on technology, data analytics, finance, digital systems, and supply chain planning. Costco joins McDonald's, American Airlines, and Eli Lilly in a series of high-profile first-ever India GCC entries into Hyderabad in 2025–2026.
deals.why: Strategic Implication: Retail sector GCCs are accelerating into India at scale—GCC leaders from consumer-facing industries should benchmark Costco's operational scope and skill mix against their own capability roadmaps to stay competitive.

deals.title: Albertsons expands Bengaluru GCC, plans 1,000 AI and data hires over 18 months
deals.body: US grocery giant Albertsons plans to hire 1,000 professionals for its Bengaluru GCC over the next 18 months, scaling from approximately 300 currently. Hiring will focus on product management, data science, AI engineering, LLM fine-tuning, and conversational AI for digital shopping experiences. Albertsons' India team holds a 'make in India for global markets' mandate with direct impact on customer-facing AI systems across the company's 2,200+ US stores.
deals.why: Strategic Implication: Retail GCCs with direct consumer-facing AI mandates are commanding premium AI talent—GCC leaders in similar sectors should evaluate whether their talent acquisition pipelines have the depth to support this intensity of AI engineering hiring.

deals.title: Daikin launches India GCC in Gurgaon with EY partnership, 700 initial hires planned
deals.body: Daikin has launched the Daikin Innovation and Services Hub GCC in Gurugram in partnership with EY, which is leading talent acquisition, system design, and operating model definition. The centre will initially employ 700 professionals across IT, digital services, data management, finance, and AI. Daikin—which recorded Rs 12,500 crore in India revenue in FY25—is also investing Rs 2,500 crore in a new export-oriented manufacturing unit, signalling a multi-dimensional India commitment.
deals.why: Strategic Implication: Industrial OEMs partnering with global consultancies to establish GCCs is an accelerating trend—GCC leaders should assess whether a consulting-led build model offers faster capability acquisition versus organic hiring for their own expansion scenarios.

deals.title: EY and Daikin: consulting-led GCC build model targets digital, AI, and finance capabilities
deals.body: EY India's GCC build engagement with Daikin—setting up the Daikin Innovation and Services Hub in Gurgaon—highlights a growing model where global consultancies act as GCC architects, hiring partners, and operating model designers from day one. EY is co-creating the centre's capability roadmap across information technology, digital services, AI, finance, and data management. This marks a departure from the traditional GCC model where parent company HR and operations teams independently build out India centres.
deals.why: Strategic Implication: Consultancy-led GCC setup models compress time-to-value but create dependency risks—GCC leaders considering accelerated builds should negotiate knowledge transfer milestones and internal capability development into any consultancy partnership.

risks.title: Zinnov: High-performer attrition at 16.5% in India GCCs, rising above overall average
risks.body: Zinnov's SIAH 2026 report reveals India GCC high-performer attrition has risen to 16.5%—above the overall attrition average and climbing from a low of 15.1% in 2024. The report warns that aggregate attrition figures mask a more damaging trend where the top performers who define GCC quality over the next three years are departing at disproportionately high rates. GCCs using blended headcount attrition numbers without segmenting high-performer cohorts are misdiagnosing the problem.
risks.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders must separate high-performer retention data from aggregate attrition reporting and build differentiated compensation, recognition, and career architecture for top-quartile talent—generic retention programmes will not address this risk.

risks.title: India faces 1.4–1.9M digital talent shortfall; critical 41% AI/data skills gap in GCCs
risks.body: NASSCOM projections indicate India will face a shortage of 1.4–1.9 million digitally skilled professionals unless organisations dramatically accelerate skilling initiatives. Within GCCs specifically, there is a reported 41% gap in AI/data skills, a 90% shortage in GenAI-ready talent, and a 55–60% deficit in cloud-native expertise. The 'missing middle' problem—a critical gap in professionals with 8–15 years of experience combining technical depth with cross-functional leadership—is becoming the binding constraint on GCC capability upgrades.
risks.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders should launch internal AI and cloud reskilling academies now; external hiring alone cannot close a 41% skills gap at scale—partnerships with universities and NASSCOM skilling platforms are essential to build the pipeline.

risks.title: GCC leadership readiness gap: India built technical depth but not enough global mandate holders
risks.body: Research highlights a structural leadership readiness gap in India GCCs—the country has built world-class technical depth but has not developed sufficient leaders capable of holding senior global mandates, managing cross-border stakeholders, and making enterprise-wide strategic decisions with full accountability. Shortages are concentrated in mid-to-senior professionals (8–15 years of experience) who possess both technical and cross-functional leadership skills. This gap is becoming a ceiling on GCC mandate expansion from global headquarters.
risks.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders should invest in structured global leadership development programmes—international rotations, P&L responsibility exposure, and executive coaching—to build the pipeline that justifies expanding global mandates to India.

risks.title: India GCC ecosystem reaches $98.4B revenue; 506 Forbes Global 2000 companies now in India
risks.body: The NASSCOM-Zinnov GCC Value Orbit report confirms India's GCC ecosystem has reached $98.4 billion in total market revenue with 506 of the Forbes Global 2000 companies operating GCCs in the country as of FY26. The GCC count has grown 32% since FY2021. With nearly half of all post-2021 GCCs built with AI at core and India now leading the global AI mandate for enterprises, the ecosystem is at an inflection point of value creation unprecedented since GCCs first emerged.
risks.why: Strategic Implication: India's GCC ecosystem credibility has never been stronger—GCC leaders should use this landmark data in strategic reviews with global CEOs and boards to justify expanded investment, elevated mandates, and larger AI budgets.

risks.title: Deloitte: India GCC sector can generate $470–600B net economic impact by FY2030
risks.body: Deloitte India's July 3 report projects India's GCC sector holds potential to generate US$470–600 billion in net economic impact by FY2030, contributing 2.2–2.8% to India's GDP and creating 20–25 million jobs. Tier-2 cities including Ahmedabad, Jaipur, Coimbatore, Kochi, and Indore are gaining traction as cost-competitive expansion destinations with specialised talent pools and supportive state policies.
risks.why: Strategic Implication: The scale of India's GCC growth runway is exceptional—GCC leaders with multi-year expansion plans should lock in real estate and talent pipelines in tier-2 cities before competition for assets intensifies as projected.

risks.title: EY: GCC-led GenAI deployments show ROI via cycle time reductions up to 70% in finance
risks.body: EY India's AIdea of India 2026 report finds that GCCs achieving the strongest returns have moved from pilots to scaled enterprise deployments with clear productivity metrics. Finance functions using autonomous agentic systems are reporting process cycle time reductions of up to 70%. India's combination of AI engineering talent and existing enterprise process knowledge gives GCCs a structural advantage in capturing this productivity upside, with 91% of leaders citing deployment speed as the key differentiator in AI investment decisions.
risks.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders should benchmark their AI deployment timeline against industry fast-movers and establish measurable ROI metrics for each GenAI workload—those presenting concrete productivity data to global leadership will secure the next round of AI investment capital.

competitor.title: Japan surpasses all APAC nations in India GCC count; AI, embedded systems, cloud lead mandate
competitor.body: Deloitte India's 'India's Strategic GCC Play for Japanese Enterprises' report finds Japan now leads Asia Pacific with 100+ GCCs in India representing 5–6% of the ecosystem. These centres are rapidly evolving into product R&D, AI, embedded systems, cloud, fintech, and digital manufacturing hubs. Tier-2 cities—Ahmedabad, Jaipur, Coimbatore, Kochi, and Indore—are seeing accelerated Japanese GCC entry driven by cost competitiveness and specialised talent.
competitor.why: Strategic Implication: Japanese industrial and automotive GCCs building advanced engineering capability in India will compete for the same AI, embedded systems, and cloud talent pool—GCC leaders in manufacturing and industrials must elevate their employer brand and mandate depth to stay competitive.

competitor.title: Google and Microsoft India data center projects face farmer backlash over land and water use
competitor.body: Rest of World reporting reveals that Google's $15 billion AI data center campus in Andhra Pradesh and Microsoft's India data center projects are facing organised farmer resistance over land acquisition pressures, water consumption concerns, and energy use in agricultural areas. The government's incentive package for Google—including tax exemptions, land allocation, and water/power reimbursements totalling Rs 22,002 crore over 20 years—is drawing public scrutiny, potentially complicating hyperscaler expansion timelines that GCCs depend on.
competitor.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders dependent on hyperscaler cloud infrastructure in India should monitor data center capacity timelines—community and regulatory friction could delay expansion; diversifying across cloud regions and providers mitigates concentration risk.

competitor.title: Bihar courts Oracle, Salesforce, Honeywell for GCC investments with Rs 25,000 crore AI plan
competitor.body: Bihar's Government directly engaged Oracle, Salesforce, Honeywell, Adobe, NetApp, and Trend Micro for GCC investments at a July 3 Bengaluru roadshow. The state unveiled a Rs 25,000 crore AI and technology infrastructure plan alongside the Bihar GCC Policy 2026 and Bihar AI Policy 2026. This adds Bihar to a growing list of state governments—including Telangana, Karnataka, Maharashtra, and UP—actively competing with preferential policies and infrastructure commitments to attract global GCC mandates.
competitor.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders from Oracle, Salesforce, and Honeywell being actively courted by Bihar signals rising state competition—existing GCC leaders should proactively renegotiate incentive packages with current state governments before new entrants secure better terms.

competitor.title: NVIDIA India: Wipro, Infosys, TechM, Persistent build enterprise agentic AI platforms at scale
competitor.body: NVIDIA's India partner blog reveals that Wipro (via WEGA platform), Infosys (via Topaz Fabric with a new NeMo-built small language model), Tech Mahindra, and Persistent Systems are deploying enterprise AI agent solutions at scale using NVIDIA AI Enterprise. Wipro's AI-agent solution is already live with a major US healthcare insurer, reshaping member experience operations. These Indian systems integrators are now co-developing the agentic AI infrastructure layer that global enterprises—and GCCs—will depend on for transformation.
competitor.why: Strategic Implication: Indian IT service partner ecosystems are building proprietary agentic AI platforms that GCCs can rapidly adopt—evaluating Wipro WEGA, Infosys Topaz, and similar platforms as accelerators for GCC AI transformation is now a practical near-term decision.

competitor.title: CBRE: Coimbatore set to lead next wave of GCC expansion in South India beyond Bengaluru
competitor.body: CBRE India research identifies Coimbatore as the leading emerging destination for GCC expansion in South India, with State Street, Accenture, Amazon, Bosch, and Cognizant already operational at India Land Tech Park. Lower attrition rates versus tier-1 metros, competitive commercial real estate, and a growing engineering talent base make Coimbatore a compelling alternative to Bengaluru and Hyderabad saturation. CBRE projects sustained GCC demand growth in this corridor through 2028.
competitor.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders evaluating hub expansion should place Coimbatore on the strategic site selection shortlist—first-mover advantage in talent pipelines and real estate costs will erode within 12–18 months as the city becomes mainstream.

talent.title: NASSCOM-Zinnov: GCC hiring rises 11%; 4.25–4.5 lakh new jobs expected in FY26
talent.body: The NASSCOM-Zinnov GCC Landscape Report confirms overall GCC hiring has risen 11% with 4.25–4.5 lakh new jobs expected in FY26 alone, on a path to 1 million new jobs by 2030. Hiring strategy has shifted decisively—organisations are prioritising targeted hiring for business-critical roles across ER&D, AI/ML, cybersecurity, data, and product engineering, with volume transactional hiring replaced by skills-first acquisition models. AI hiring demand has grown 1.5 percentage points in the past six months.
talent.why: Strategic Implication: GCC talent leaders who have not yet transitioned from headcount-based hiring plans to capability-density models will struggle to compete for the AI engineering talent that is reshaping the GCC value proposition.

talent.title: Zinnov: High-performer attrition rising to 16.5%; salary concentration among deep-tech roles accelerates
talent.body: Zinnov's India GCC Salary Increase, Attrition, and Hiring Trends 2026 report reveals that overall attrition has stabilized at approximately 16%, but high-performer attrition has risen to 16.5% and is climbing. Salary increases are being concentrated among deep-tech roles—AI engineering, cloud architecture, and cybersecurity—rather than distributed equally, reflecting scarcity premiums for hard-to-fill positions. Niche AI, cloud, and cybersecurity roles now command 1.5–2x standard engineering compensation across India GCCs.
talent.why: Strategic Implication: GCC compensation committees must move to role-specific benchmarking and dynamic pay bands for AI/cloud/security talent—across-the-board salary cycles systematically undercompensate the most critical roles and accelerate high-performer attrition.

talent.title: GCC talent market 2026: niche skills at 1.7x premium; 1 in 4 roles to be contractual
talent.body: Taggd's GCC Talent Market 2026 report finds niche skills in AI engineering, GenAI architecture, cloud platforms, and cybersecurity command salary premiums of 1.7x over standard engineering roles. One in four GCC positions is expected to be contractual by year-end as organisations shift to agile talent models for specialised capabilities. Roles in AI/ML, data engineering, cybersecurity, and cloud architecture remain the hardest to fill with the longest time-to-hire, with a 41% reported gap between demand and available AI/data talent.
talent.why: Strategic Implication: GCC workforce planning must account for a permanent premium talent segment—building a managed contractual talent channel for specialised AI and security roles alongside a permanent core is now a structural necessity, not a stopgap.

talent.title: India GCC talent gap: 90% shortage in GenAI-ready professionals, 55-60% deficit in cloud-native
talent.body: Research on India's GCC talent landscape shows 90% shortage in GenAI-ready talent, a 55–60% deficit in cloud-native expertise, and a 25–30% shortfall in mid-to-senior cybersecurity specialists. Bengaluru leads in cloud-native platform, cybersecurity, and SRE talent, while Hyderabad offers strong cloud, data, and enterprise engineering talent with lower attrition. Both cities are seeing above-market salary premiums for AI and security engineers that are compressing GCC cost-efficiency metrics.
talent.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders in cybersecurity and cloud-native platform domains face the most acute talent competition—multi-year university partnerships and internal apprenticeship pipelines for these disciplines should be launched as strategic initiatives, not HR programmes.

talent.title: India GCC to create 4.25–4.5 lakh jobs in FY26; fresher hiring shifting to AI-first profiles
talent.body: V3 Staffing's GCC hiring trends analysis confirms India's GCCs will create 4.25–4.5 lakh new jobs in FY26, with approximately 400,000 fresher roles expected by 2030 as the ecosystem scales to 3 million workers. Fresh hiring is increasingly concentrated in AI-adjacent roles, with organisations preferring AI-fluent graduates over traditional software engineering generalists. Campus hiring strategies at top engineering schools are being redesigned to prioritise data science, AI, and platform engineering curricula.
talent.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders should establish dedicated campus relationships at IITs, NITs, and emerging technical universities focused specifically on AI/data disciplines—early placement partnerships now will secure differentiated fresher pipelines as competition intensifies.

policy.title: DPDPA Phase 2 countdown: Consent Manager provisions enforceable November 13, 2026
policy.body: India's Digital Personal Data Protection Act Phase 2 deadline is November 13, 2026—just four months away—when Consent Manager provisions become operative. GCCs acting as data processors for overseas parent companies' customers benefit from the Section 17(1)(d) exemption for cross-border data processing under contract. However, GCCs must separately comply as data fiduciaries for employee, vendor, and Indian customer data. Penalties for non-compliance reach Rs 250 crore for serious violations including unauthorised processing and breach non-reporting.
policy.why: Strategic Implication: GCC legal and compliance leaders must immediately audit which data flows fall under fiduciary versus processor status and have consent architectures, data inventories, and breach response protocols operational before November 2026—not in 2027.

policy.title: Bihar GCC Policy 2026: Rs 25,000 crore tech infrastructure; state competition for GCC investment intensifies
policy.body: Bihar formally activated the Bihar GCC Policy 2026 at its July 3 Bengaluru roadshow, offering a Rs 25,000 crore AI and technology infrastructure commitment alongside the Bihar AI Policy 2026. The policy—originally approved February 5, 2026—targets global brands including Oracle, Salesforce, Adobe, and Honeywell. Bihar joins Telangana, Karnataka, Maharashtra, and Uttar Pradesh in a competitive race for GCC mandates, raising the benchmark for state-level policy packages across India.
policy.why: Strategic Implication: Intensifying state competition for GCC mandates creates a strong negotiating environment for GCC leaders—existing location commitments should be reviewed to ensure current incentive packages remain competitive versus what new entrants are receiving.

policy.title: India 2026 Union Budget: 21-year tax holiday for cloud data centers to 2047 enables GCC AI infrastructure
policy.body: India's Union Budget 2026–27 enacted a tax holiday through 2047 for foreign cloud service companies operating India-based data centres for global customers, coupled with a 15.5% safe harbour margin for IT services. The measures target Microsoft, Google, and Amazon to build out AI computing infrastructure in India. Google's $15B Andhra Pradesh AI campus and Microsoft's multi-site India data center programme are direct outcomes of this policy framework, directly enhancing the cloud AI infrastructure available to India-based GCCs.
policy.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders with significant AI infrastructure requirements should model India-based cloud workload costs against the 2047 tax structure—the policy creates a strong long-term economic case for running AI training and inference workloads from India.

policy.title: Grant Thornton: Emerging GCC tax risks—transfer pricing, PE exposure, GST on intra-group services
policy.body: Grant Thornton India identifies three emerging GCC tax risk areas for 2026: transfer pricing disputes on IP contribution and DEMPE functions as GCCs take on higher-value mandates; Permanent Establishment exposure where India GCCs exercise independent strategic decision-making; and GST applicability on services supplied by Indian GCCs to foreign group entities. As GCCs shift from cost-plus service providers to risk-and-reward participants, their tax and compliance profile is fundamentally changing.
policy.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders who have expanded mandates without a parallel review of their transfer pricing documentation and PE risk exposure are accumulating regulatory liability—a proactive GCC tax health check is now urgent given the increased mandate complexity.

policy.title: India-GCC FTA negotiations launched; bilateral trade at $178.6B signals strategic alignment
policy.body: India and the Gulf Cooperation Council formally launched Free Trade Agreement negotiations after finalising Terms of Reference in February 2026, with bilateral trade reaching US$178.56 billion in FY2024–25 representing 15.42% of India's global trade. For India's tech GCC sector, an India-GCC FTA would facilitate cross-border data flows, expand market access for GCC-delivered digital services, and ease professional mobility between Gulf markets and India.
policy.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders with BFSI, insurance, or digital services mandates serving Middle East markets should track FTA progress closely—FTA provisions on digital trade and services could create new delivery channel opportunities and reduce compliance friction for Gulf-facing workloads.

tech.title: NASSCOM-Zinnov: 58% GCCs investing in Agentic AI; 29% planning adoption within one year
tech.body: The NASSCOM-Zinnov GCC Landscape 2026 Report confirms 58% of India GCCs are actively investing in Agentic AI capabilities, with another 29% planning investments within a year—indicating near-universal enterprise AI commitment within 12 months. 83% are already scaling GenAI. The most successful GCCs are deploying a hybrid agentic operating model where AI handles continuous execution and workflow orchestration. Finance cycle times are down 70%, customer service automation is at 65% penetration, and IT/cybersecurity operations are 45% GenAI-enabled.
tech.why: Strategic Implication: With 87% of GCCs either deployed or planning Agentic AI within one year, GCC technology leaders who have not yet established an Agentic AI architecture blueprint are operationally behind—prioritising a 90-day Agentic AI readiness assessment is urgent.

tech.title: EY AIdea of India 2026: GCC GenAI ROI—deployment speed is key; 70% cycle time reductions in finance
tech.body: EY India's AIdea of India 2026 report on enterprise GenAI ROI finds 91% of GCC leaders cite deployment speed as the primary factor in buy-vs-build decisions, with 24% already deploying Agentic AI in production and enterprise agentic AI adoption in India having reached 70% as of mid-2026. Finance and CX functions are delivering the strongest measured ROI, with cycle time reductions of up to 70% in accounts payable, claims processing, and customer interaction resolution. ROI is no longer theoretical—it is measurable and being delivered at scale.
tech.why: Strategic Implication: GCC technology leaders presenting AI business cases to global boards must include concrete ROI metrics from comparable deployments—abstract AI transformation narratives no longer unlock investment; quantified cycle-time and cost reductions do.

tech.title: GitHub Copilot reaches 4.7M enterprise subscribers at 90% of Fortune 100; India GCC ROI is 10x
tech.body: GitHub Copilot has reached 4.7 million paid subscribers with 75% year-over-year growth, deployed at approximately 90% of Fortune 100 companies. Enterprise deployments report developers are 55% more productive in code writing, and pull request cycle times have dropped from 9.6 days to 2.4 days. At ₹1,895/user/month for Indian GCCs on the Business plan, productivity savings of one hour per developer per week delivers approximately 10x ROI, making developer AI tooling one of the highest-return technology investments available to GCC engineering leaders.
tech.why: Strategic Implication: GCC engineering leaders who have not yet mandated a developer AI tooling standard (GitHub Copilot, Cursor, or equivalent) are leaving measurable productivity gains unrealised—a tool standardisation decision and rollout plan should be on the Q3 2026 engineering leadership agenda.

tech.title: India IndiaAI Mission: INR 10,000 crore sovereign AI fund and 40,000 GPUs for domestic AI infrastructure
tech.body: India's IndiaAI Mission is backed by over INR 10,000 crore and a commitment to deploy 40,000 GPUs to build independent domestic AI compute infrastructure, with analysts estimating the initiative could add up to US$1 trillion to India's economy by 2035. The mission enables Indian GCCs to run AI model training and inference on domestic infrastructure, improving data residency compliance and reducing dependency on overseas hyperscaler compute. NASSCOM identifies this as a defining moment for India's tech leadership in global AI.
tech.why: Strategic Implication: GCC technology leaders should evaluate IndiaAI Mission compute allocation for AI training workloads—early access to sovereign GPU infrastructure offers cost advantages and a strategic data localisation positioning benefit for global board narratives.

tech.title: Agentic AI enterprise adoption at 72% in production globally; governance gap remains a critical risk
tech.body: The Agentic AI Institute's 2026 enterprise adoption report finds 72% of enterprise AI deployments globally are now in production rather than pilot, with India's GCCs identified as a leading implementation geography due to concentration of AI engineering talent and process knowledge. A critical governance gap persists: 68% of enterprises report insufficient AI governance frameworks to manage autonomous agent decisions, creating compliance and liability risk as agentic systems take on higher-stakes business processes including finance, HR, and customer data.
tech.why: Strategic Implication: GCC leaders must pair Agentic AI deployment acceleration with a formal AI governance framework—autonomous agent decisions touching finance, legal, HR, or customer data require documented oversight protocols before regulators establish mandatory standards that may be harder to meet retroactively.
